title: The Human Specification Accord: A Novel Framework for Global Biotechnology Governance
author: Luberisse, Joshua
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Advances in CRISPR-based editing, neural interfaces, and programmable organisms have moved biotechnology into an era of high-impact, dual-use capability that diffuses at cloud speed. Yet the world lacks an inspection and verification regime proportional to that power. This paper proposes the Human Specification Accord (HSA): a layered governance architecture that couples (i) a gene-editing inspectorate modeled on successful elements of nuclear safeguards to (ii) a neurorights charter protecting mental privacy, cognitive liberty, algorithmic auditability, and data portability. The HSA operationalizes transparency through a permissioned digital chain-of custody ledger for reagents and designs; material traceability via reagent fingerprinting; tiered routine–challenge–snap inspections tied to market access; and cloud-licensing levers that make noncompliance economically costly. We map legal hooks across trade, biosecurity, and human rights law; simulate compliance pathways for research and clinical settings; and analyze evasion risks alongside mitigation. By relocating a meaningful share of verification into supply chains and compute stacks, the HSA translates ethical intention into institutional power without stalling scientific discovery.

The HSA operationalizes transparency through a permissioned digital chain-of custody ledger for reagents and designs; material traceability via reagent fingerprinting; tiered routine–challenge–snap inspections tied to market access; and cloud-licensing levers that make noncompliance economically costly. We map legal hooks across trade, biosecurity, and human rights law; simulate compliance pathways for research and clinical settings; and analyze evasion risks alongside mitigation.
